items.q: Which orchestral family does the saxophone belong to?
items.misconception: Brass (because it is made of brass).
items.why: Students classify by material. Instrument families are defined by SOUND PRODUCTION: woodwinds use a reed or air across an edge. The sax uses a reed, so despite the brass body, it's woodwind.

items.q: How many semitones are in a perfect fifth?
items.why: Students count fifth as 'five steps' but the name refers to letter NAMES (1-2-3-4-5), not semitones. C to G spans 5 letter names but 7 semitones (C-C#-D-D#-E-F-F#-G).

items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.
Correct answer: Common time. “Rare time” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many quaver beats per bar in 6/8?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
